title: Situational Answer Patterns
summary: A situation-to-answer cheat sheet built from mock-question review: for each classic scenario, the best PMP action and the lesson behind it, grouped by knowledge area. Harmonised with the PMP mindset so the 'best first action' is consistent throughout.

sections.heading: Stakeholder management
sections.body:
- Stakeholders were uninformed during a sprint review / important meeting -> review the Communications Management Plan first. Check what the plan already says before assuming new analysis is needed.
- A key stakeholder is replaced mid-execution -> update the Stakeholder Register, then re-check the engagement approach for the new person.
- A stakeholder complains documentation is missing (agile) -> acknowledge the concern, clarify the real need (which docs, why), then provide it. Don't dismiss or redirect.
- High-influence stakeholders hold conflicting expectations -> elicit and negotiate the requirements, weighting by influence, without compromising the approved project objectives.
- A previously unidentified stakeholder appears -> add them to the Stakeholder Register and analyse them FIRST, before actioning any request they make.
sections.tip:
Uninformed stakeholders -> check the Communications Management Plan before acting.
New or changed stakeholder -> the Register holds the who; the Engagement Plan holds the how.

sections.heading: When the functional manager pulls resources
sections.body:
A functional manager reassigning your people is a resource conflict, not an automatic escalation.

- First, negotiate directly with the functional manager to protect the commitment.
- If collaboration fails and the project is genuinely being harmed, escalate to senior management (or the sponsor / steering committee for governance-level authority gaps).
- Proactive scheduling actions (resource leveling / smoothing) can absorb short-term availability changes rather than just logging the issue.
sections.tip: Talk to the functional manager FIRST; escalate only when negotiation fails. Jumping straight to 'escalate' is the classic distractor.

sections.heading: Risk management
sections.body:
- A previously identified risk materialises -> update the Risk Register first (status / probability / impact), then move to issue or change handling.
- A risk with a planned response fires -> consult the risk response plan (and contract / SOW) and apply the planned response; don't improvise a new one.
- A significant delay with downstream effects -> analyse the business impact and reprioritise deliverables before jumping to mitigation.
- Resources removed during execution create a schedule risk -> request resource leveling as a proactive scheduling response.
sections.tip: Document the change in the Register before raising issues or change requests. Always assess impact before choosing a mitigation.

sections.heading: Change control & scope
sections.body:
- A submitted change request whose delay will trigger bigger problems -> revise the change request so it reflects the full, current impact before it is evaluated.
- Important features were dropped in scope negotiations and are now causing problems -> work WITH the client to negotiate including the missing features; attempt resolution at the client level before escalating.
- Remember: any change to a baseline (scope / schedule / cost) runs through Integrated Change Control - raise a change request, assess impact, let the CCB decide, then update. Registers and logs update freely without one.
sections.tip: Keep the change request complete and current. Resolve scope gaps collaboratively with the client first, escalate second.

sections.heading: Agile & hybrid practice
sections.body:
- Introducing story points to a Kanban team -> estimate the NEW backlog items only first; don't stop to re-point existing work in progress. (Establishing a shared baseline matters, but you get it from new items, not by disrupting current WIP.)
- Separate design and execution teams causing friction on a hybrid project -> establish a centralised repository for documentation and validation to improve knowledge sharing, rather than assuming training is needed.
- Stakeholders uninformed during sprint reviews -> update artifacts frequently and transparently and lean on lightweight collaboration tools; agile favours real-time sharing over rigid reporting.
- A team member keeps picking easy / low-priority stories -> have a private conversation to understand the root cause before assuming a skills gap or issuing directives.
- Users are uncomfortable with features from the last iteration -> obtain stakeholder feedback and team insights before acting.
- A stakeholder wants to reorder deliverables mid-project -> bring them into sprint / iteration planning to re-prioritise, rather than using formal change control.
- A client wants two modules earlier than planned -> hold a release-planning meeting to re-plan delivery.
- Developers are frustrated by standups and story updates -> acknowledge the frustration first, then explain the value each ceremony provides (servant leadership).
- The team has productivity ideas during an agile transition -> capture and act on them in retrospectives, the primary forum for process improvement.
- Assessing roadblocks before a milestone (Kanban) -> review the blocked items with the team in the daily standup.
sections.tip: Agile default: diagnose WITH the team, empower the Product Owner on priority, improve the process in the retro, and start estimating with new work only.

sections.heading: User stories - INVEST
sections.body:
Well-written, right-sized user stories satisfy INVEST:

- Independent.
- Negotiable.
- Valuable.
- Estimable.
- Small.
- Testable.

Oversized or vague functionality -> write detailed stories and refine the backlog iteratively; don't fall back on heavyweight requirements documents.
sections.tip: INVEST = Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable. Value + testability are what make a story 'done-able'.

sections.heading: Governance, initiation & handover
sections.body:
- Taking over a chaotic project (negative variances, unresolved issues, unpaid vendors) -> the original PM should have documented assumptions in the charter and identified high-level risks early. Strong early planning prevents most later pain.
- The client's PM lacks financial authority and is causing delays -> escalate to the steering committee; authority and governance gaps belong at the governance level.
- A team leader bypasses the PM to talk directly to the sponsor -> remind them of the existing communication ground rule first (address the violation directly). Only if it recurs do you strengthen the agreement to require PM approval.
- A compatibility / technical issue surfaces in initiation -> check the lessons-learned database and other organisational process assets before anything else.
- A stakeholder wants to pause the project to assess a new process -> consult the sponsor to determine next steps; strategic direction changes are the sponsor's call.
sections.tip: Address a ground-rule violation directly before changing the process. Strategic pause / direction -> sponsor. Authority gap -> steering committee.

sections.heading: Cost, schedule & estimating
sections.body:
- Senior management wants a status report on an over-budget project -> compare actual results to the plan (variance analysis); focus on current performance data.
- Under a financial crisis, management must decide whether to continue -> assess economic feasibility with a cost-benefit analysis (BCR). Below 1, costs outweigh benefits and you consult the sponsor.
- A likely multi-month delay -> analyse the business impact and reprioritise deliverables before choosing a mitigation.
- Difficulty estimating activity durations -> use historical data from similar past projects (analogous estimating); for a realistic estimate on ongoing work, ask the people doing the work, and for a new/specialised area ask domain experts.
sections.tip: Status report -> variance analysis. Continue-or-not decision -> cost-benefit. Always assess impact before mitigating.

sections.heading: Team management & leadership
sections.body:
- New team members underperforming under a tight timeline -> pair them with experienced members via job shadowing / peer mentoring; fast practical knowledge transfer beats formal training under pressure.
- Low morale and inefficiency with an unknown cause -> run a satisfaction evaluation first; diagnose before implementing team-building or incentives.
- A new PM inherits a team with friction from the previous manager -> observe and interact to assess the team culture before making changes.
- A team member violates an agreed ground rule -> remind them of the existing agreement directly.
- A new team member is uneasy about untested work after a handover -> ensure a documented knowledge-transfer / handover exists; documentation is more reliable than relying on a prior sign-off.
sections.tip: Diagnose before treating: survey the team (whole-team issue) or hold a one-on-one (individual issue) before jumping to a fix.

sections.heading: Communications
sections.body:
- A team member prepares a report in the wrong format for the CEO -> use interactive (two-way) communication to confirm understanding; confirmation beats one-way instruction.
- Stakeholders are uninformed at important meetings -> review the Communications Management Plan first.
- Important information bypassed the PM -> reinforce the existing ground rules with a direct reminder before changing the process.
- Methods: interactive (meetings / calls, best for complex topics), push (emails / reports), pull (portals / wikis).
sections.tip: Confirm understanding interactively for anything complex or high-stakes. Address process gaps by updating the plan, not resending it.

sections.heading: Procurement, closure & funding
sections.body:
- Several contracts no longer align with company objectives -> recommend terminating the misaligned ones through the proper channels.
- A client asks to spend leftover project budget on operational activities after handover -> reject the request and close the project financials; contingency reserves and project funds don't cover post-project operations.
- Defects are found during closing -> review the deliverables against the acceptance criteria first, before any other action.
- Closure order: confirm deliverables are accepted -> close contracts / procurement -> capture lessons learned -> release the team last. Don't close until finance confirms all payments.
sections.tip: Verify against acceptance criteria before acting on a closing defect. Project money can't fund operations - close the financials.

sections.heading: General patterns & exam tips
sections.body:
- Diagnose before treating: assess (satisfaction survey, power/interest grid, variance / impact analysis) before jumping to a solution.
- Follow the process: don't bypass formal change control or governance, especially around a baseline.
- Agile vs predictive mindset: in high-change / agile settings favour lightweight, frequent, collaborative moves; in predictive settings favour formal process and change requests.
- Influence matters: high-influence stakeholders need more negotiation and attention - but never at the cost of the approved objectives.
- Early planning prevents later pain: clear assumptions and early risk identification are the most-cited preventive measures.
- Least-drastic correct action: prefer talking to the functional manager over escalating, reminding of a ground rule over rewriting the process, and diagnosing with the team over deciding for them.
sections.tip: Most ties break the same way: the proactive option that assesses first, follows process, and puts the right role in charge wins.

source: Mock-question review (July 2026), harmonised with the PMP mindset and Brad's master cheat sheet
